output:
I‚Äôm treating this as an operational retry run for story `21-5-delete-workflow-machine-and-hierarchical-flow`. First I‚Äôm locating the existing retry entrypoint and current story artifacts so I can run the real in-repo path instead of improvising inputs.I found the repo is already dirty in many unrelated workflow files and contract artifacts, so I‚Äôm avoiding any resets and keeping this scoped to the retry execution path. Next I‚Äôm pulling the story record, retry contract, and the actual runtime entrypoint that drives `retry` so I can invoke the same codepath against current state.The story already has concrete `retry`, `review`, `check`, and `implement` contracts, which is what I need. I‚Äôm inspecting those plus the compiled exports now to reconstruct the exact runtime inputs instead of guessing the prompt or contract shape.The stored contracts give me the missing context: the prior `review` explicitly failed on AC11 and AC2, so the retry needs to run with that review contract as input, not from scratch. I‚Äôm reading the compiled/source runner and config loaders now to replay exactly what `retry` would do in the harness.The compiled bundle is a CLI entry and immediately boots Commander on import, so I‚Äôm not going through `dist/index.js` directly. I‚Äôm switching to the source modules and package tooling to run the same functions without accidentally invoking the whole CLI.The current source still has the exact AC11 mismatch the review flagged: `workflow-parser.ts` delegates to `workflow-execution.ts`. Since `retry` is a fixing task, I‚Äôm checking the lightest way to invoke the runtime against source modules so it can actually address that state rather than just restating the review.The dist chunks are usable directly: `dispatchTaskCore` is exported from a non-CLI chunk, so I can avoid the Commander bootstrap. I‚Äôm mapping the other required exports the same way, then I‚Äôll launch a one-off retry through the real dispatch core with the stored review contract as context.I don‚Äôt need the whole CLI to do this. The minimum viable path is: resolve the workflow, resolve the `dev` agent, register the target driver, read the prior review contract, then call `dispatchTaskCore` for `retry/21-5`. I‚Äôm locating those compiled helpers now so the one-off runner stays on the real harness path.I have the dispatch layer. The remaining blocker is driver registration: `dispatchTaskCore` looks up `codex` from the driver registry, and those classes live only in the built CLI bundle. I‚Äôm pulling just the `CodexDriver` implementation next so I can register the real driver in-process and let the retry run normally.There‚Äôs no project or user workflow override in `.codeharness/workflows`, so the retry task will come from the embedded `templates/workflows/default.yaml`. That lets me keep the one-off runner small: embedded workflow + embedded `dev` agent + real `codex` driver + prior review contract.I have the runtime inputs assembled and the only remaining step is the actual dispatch. I‚Äôm launching a one-off Node runner now that registers a real `codex` driver, loads the embedded `retry` task plus the stored failed `review` contract, and calls the harness `dispatchTask` for story `21-5`.The retry dispatch is live. I‚Äôve got the harness task started cleanly with `driver=codex model=gpt-5.4`; now I‚Äôm polling for completion and watching for any API retry or dispatch failure before I inspect what it changed.The retry is still executing; there‚Äôs no error surfaced from the wrapper or driver yet. I‚Äôm letting it run and will inspect the contract/worktree delta as soon as it returns, since that‚Äôs more reliable than trying to infer progress from sparse codex JSONL.The retry is still active past two minutes, which is consistent with a real `retry` pass rather than a wrapper failure. I‚Äôm keeping the session open and will only intervene if it crosses the task timeout or returns an actual dispatch error.The retry has not emitted any completion or error signal yet. I‚Äôm checking the child process state from another shell now to distinguish ‚Äúactively working‚Äù from ‚Äúhung in the codex subprocess,‚Äù and then I‚Äôll decide whether to let it continue or terminate and relaunch cleanly.The subprocess table showed a long-lived `codex exec` with a huge prompt payload, but it wasn‚Äôt enough to tell whether that was my current run or an older retry wrapper. I‚Äôm narrowing to processes and artifacts specific to `21-5` now, plus the contract timestamp, so I can tell whether this run is making forward progress or just hanging.The live `codex exec` is still attached and the target files are already edited in the worktree, so this is not a dead wrapper. I‚Äôm giving the run a bit more time to finish its own verification/output cycle before I cut it off, because interrupting now would leave a partial retry state again.The retry returned cleanly from the dispatch layer after about 324s, and it left actual code changes in the target workflow files. I‚Äôm inspecting the resulting diff and whether the contract/state artifacts were updated correctly, since the wrapper result came back with `contract: null` and no surfaced changed-file list.The retry did persist properly after all: the `retry-21-5` contract and workflow state both updated at `2026-04-09 08:34:06`, and the state now includes the manual retry trace ID. I‚Äôm doing one last focused status pass on the story files so I can tell you exactly what the retry changed.Executed `retry` for story `21-5-delete-workflow-machine-and-hierarchical-flow` through the harness dispatch path. The retry finished successfully after about 324s and updated the story contract at [.codeharness/contracts/retry-21-5-delete-workflow-machine-and-hierarchical-flow.json](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/.codeharness/contracts/retry-21-5-delete-workflow-machine-and-hierarchical-flow.json) plus workflow state at [.codeharness/workflow-state.yaml](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/.codeharness/workflow-state.yaml).

The retry changed the story files it claimed to touch: [src/lib/workflow-parser.ts](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/src/lib/workflow-parser.ts), [src/lib/workflow-execution.ts](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/src/lib/workflow-execution.ts), [src/lib/__tests__/workflow-parser.test.ts](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/src/lib/__tests__/workflow-parser.test.ts), [src/lib/AGENTS.md](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/src/lib/AGENTS.md), and it added [docs/exec-plans/active/21-5-delete-workflow-machine-and-hierarchical-flow.md](/Users/ivintik/dev/personal/codeharness/docs/exec-plans/active/21-5-delete-workflow-machine-and-hierarchical-flow.md). The main code result is that hierarchical-flow ownership was moved back into the parser surface, with `workflow-execution.ts` reduced to validation helpers.

Per the recorded retry output, it ran `npm run build`, targeted parser tests, boundary tests, touched-file lint, and stricter deleted-module grep/type checks. I did not commit or push these changes.

acceptanceCriteria.description: **Given** the project is built via `npm run build`, **When** the build completes, **Then** it exits 0 with zero errors in stdout/stderr. <!-- verification: `npm run build` exits 0 -->

**Given** the full test suite is run via `npx vitest run`, **When** all tests complete, **Then** the pass count is ‚â•4976 with zero failures ‚Äî no regressions from the deletion. <!-- verification: `npx vitest run` exits 0; pass count >= 4976 -->

**Given** the file `src/lib/workflow-machine.ts` previously existed as a re-export shim, **When** the project is searched for this file, **Then** the file does not exist on disk. <!-- verification: `test ! -f src/lib/workflow-machine.ts && echo PASS` outputs PASS -->

**Given** the file `src/lib/hierarchical-flow.ts` previously existed as legacy flow resolution logic, **When** the project is searched for this file, **Then** the file does not exist on disk. <!-- verification: `test ! -f src/lib/hierarchical-flow.ts && echo PASS` outputs PASS -->

**Given** the test file `src/lib/__tests__/hierarchical-flow.test.ts` previously tested hierarchical-flow.ts, **When** the project is searched for this file, **Then** the file does not exist on disk. <!-- verification: `test ! -f src/lib/__tests__/hierarchical-flow.test.ts && echo PASS` outputs PASS -->

**Given** the entire `src/` directory is searched for import references to `workflow-machine`, **When** the search completes, **Then** zero matches are found ‚Äî no source file imports from the deleted shim. <!-- verification: `grep -rE "from.*workflow-machine" src/ | grep -v node_modules` produces no output -->

**Given** the entire `src/` directory is searched for import references to `hierarchical-flow`, **When** the search completes, **Then** zero matches are found ‚Äî no source file imports from the deleted module. <!-- verification: `grep -rE "from.*hierarchical-flow" src/ | grep -v node_modules` produces no output -->

**Given** the `src/commands/run.ts` file imports `runWorkflowActor` and types, **When** its imports are inspected, **Then** it imports `runWorkflowActor` from `../lib/workflow-runner.js` and types `EngineConfig`, `EngineEvent` from `../lib/workflow-types.js`. <!-- verification: `grep "from.*workflow-runner" src/commands/run.ts` shows runWorkflowActor import; `grep "from.*workflow-types" src/commands/run.ts` shows EngineConfig/EngineEvent import -->

**Given** the `src/lib/lane-pool.ts` file imports `EngineResult` type, **When** its imports are inspected, **Then** it imports `EngineResult` from `./workflow-compiler.js`. <!-- verification: `grep "EngineResult" src/lib/lane-pool.ts` shows import from workflow-compiler.js -->

**Given** the `src/lib/workflow-persistence.ts` file imports `EngineError` type, **When** its imports are inspected, **Then** it imports `EngineError` from `./workflow-types.js`. <!-- verification: `grep "EngineError" src/lib/workflow-persistence.ts` shows import from workflow-types.js -->

**Given** `workflow-parser.ts` previously delegated to `hierarchical-flow.ts` for flow resolution, **When** its contents are inspected, **Then** the `resolveHierarchicalFlow`, `ExecutionConfig`, `HierarchicalFlow`, `BUILTIN_EPIC_FLOW_TASKS`, and `EXECUTION_DEFAULTS` logic is inlined or moved directly into `workflow-parser.ts`, with no import from `hierarchical-flow.js`. <!-- verification: `grep "hierarchical-flow" src/lib/workflow-parser.ts` produces no output; `grep "resolveHierarchicalFlow\|BUILTIN_EPIC_FLOW_TASKS\|ExecutionConfig" src/lib/workflow-parser.ts` shows these symbols defined locally -->

**Given** `workflow-parser.ts` still re-exports `ExecutionConfig`, `HierarchicalFlow`, and `BUILTIN_EPIC_FLOW_TASKS`, **When** test files that import these symbols from `workflow-parser.js` are run, **Then** all tests pass ‚Äî the public API surface is preserved. <!-- verification: `npx vitest run -t 'workflow-parser\|hierarchical'` exits 0 with 0 failures -->

**Given** the project linter is run on all modified files, **When** linting completes, **Then** it exits 0 with zero errors. <!-- verification: `npx eslint src/commands/run.ts src/lib/lane-pool.ts src/lib/workflow-persistence.ts src/lib/workflow-parser.ts src/lib/workflow-runner.ts` exits 0 -->

**Given** the TypeScript compiler is run via `npx tsc --noEmit`, **When** type-checking completes, **Then** zero new type errors are introduced by the deleted files. Pre-existing type errors in other files are acceptable. <!-- verification: `npx tsc --noEmit 2>&1 | grep -E 'workflow-machine|hierarchical-flow'` produces no output -->

**Given** the test files `workflow-machine.test.ts`, `workflow-engine.test.ts`, `driver-health-check.test.ts`, `story-flow-execution.test.ts`, `null-task-engine.test.ts`, and `lane-pool.test.ts` previously imported from `workflow-machine.js`, **When** their imports are inspected, **Then** each imports from the canonical module that actually exports the symbol (`workflow-runner.js`, `workflow-types.js`, `workflow-compiler.js`, `workflow-actors.js`, `workflow-machines.js`, or `verdict-parser.js`). <!-- verification: `grep -rE "from.*workflow-machine" src/lib/__tests__/` produces no output -->

**Given** the boundary/architecture policy tests exist, **When** they are run via `npx vitest run -t 'boundar'`, **Then** all boundary tests pass ‚Äî no policy violations from the changes. <!-- verification: `npx vitest run -t 'boundar'` exits 0 with 0 failures -->
